audits.excerpt: "No." Jaina crushed the bread heel hard enough to crack the crust. She swallowed against stale sweetness and ash and lifted her free hand with the crumbs still clinging to her fingers. "It is narrower now. Less a pull. More a line inland."
audits.reason: The carrier is better, but the line still resolves into map-room shorthand. "Less a pull. More a line inland" tells the room the conclusion instead of pinning the sensation to the visible spoor or to the body taking that tug.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep the bread, ash, and crumbs, but make her name what the ground is doing in front of them: the tug is no longer everywhere; it is running with that north-west track or those sled grooves.

audits.excerpt: "Only wind moved across the flats ahead. Even so, her own ward-sense had started complaining hours ago. The ache across the back of her teeth tightened as they pushed inland. Leave. Turn back."
audits.reason: "Ward-sense had started complaining" is still abstract, almost chatty diagnostic language. The ache in the teeth is the real grounding move; the sentence should stay there instead of naming an internal system in summary form.
audits.suggestedFix: Cut the "ward-sense...complaining" phrasing and let the warning live in the bodily tells already on the page: the ache behind her teeth, the tightening skin, the instinct to turn before the cave is even visible.

audits.excerpt: "Below them the glacier opened in a dark seam wide enough to swallow a hall... Beneath it, in Common, one half-buried line still read clear enough to land: SO MUST POWER SCAR THE SPIRIT. The words shone pale in the dark like a split vein in the glacier."
audits.reason: The inscription is finally present, but the last image tips back into decorative metaphor. This threshold warning needs to land as scraped ice, exposed letters, and cost — not as a pretty simile that softens the hard edge of the warning.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep Muradin scraping the groove and the quoted warning, then replace the "split vein" image with a colder, plainer read of the letters showing through the frost.

audits.excerpt: "They're inside the field," Jaina said, too fast. "Don't let them bracket us. And don't cast straight through the middle unless I tell you."
audits.reason: In the middle of first contact, "inside the field" is still systems-talk. The chamber has already shown the men, the angle, and the pressure; Jaina's warning should cash out in what the spell will do to the room, not in abstract combat-magic terminology.
audits.suggestedFix: Have her warn off the straight cast through the visible behavior of the room: if she throws through the middle, the cave will kick it sideways or break it before it reaches Arthas.

audits.excerpt: "Arthas stopped ten paces away. Frost spread around his boots in pale petals. He watched them without haste."
audits.reason: "Pale petals" prettifies the threat at the exact moment the blade should feel ugly and invasive. The beat needs hard frost crawling or branching over the floor, not floral imagery.
audits.suggestedFix: Replace the petal metaphor with a harsher physical spread — frost running out in thin spokes, cracks, or rime around his sabatons.

audits.excerpt: "They burst out through the cave throat with Muradin dragging between two elves, his boots carving twin trenches in the snow. Light's Vengeance still burned where it had fallen, a gold accusation under drift."
audits.reason: "Gold accusation" turns a concrete extraction decision back into authorial moral language. The hammer already carries enough charge as abandoned holy metal in the snow; this beat should stay with glow, drift, and the lack of free hands.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep the glowing hammer in the drift, but strip the moralized metaphor and let the image stay physical: gold under snow, left behind because the carriers cannot spare a hand.

audits.excerpt: "By the time the camp palisade showed below them through the whitening dark, Muradin had groaned once and gone limp again."
audits.reason: This is a source-fidelity slip. The chapter correctly grounded Muradin's position earlier as an ugly ice-block circle with a levered gate; calling it a palisade on return drifts the camp back toward generic timber fort language.
audits.suggestedFix: Replace "camp palisade" with the ice-block wall, the rough white ring, or the levered gate so the return shot matches the camp's established material identity.
